text:
Two weeks of top-level sports events at the Paris Olympics brought many moments of joy and heartache, but the 2024 Games were also tarnished by multiple incidents of online hate speech against minorities, as well as a series of religious and national disputes on social media.

Research by BIRN’s team of digital rights monitors showed that two major controversies dominated online discourse in South-East and Central Europe throughout the duration of the Games: the row over alleged blasphemy at the opening ceremony, and the involvement of female boxers Imane Khelif and Lin Yu-Ting.

Algerian pugilist Imane Khelif was the target of the harshest personal insults online. She and Lin Yu-ting of Taiwan had both previously been disqualified from 2023’s boxing World Championships by the International Boxing Association, IBA for failing gender eligibility tests. The International Olympic Committee, however,questioned the reliability of the IBA’s tests, calling them “illegitimate”.

Nevertheless, amid widespread offensive commentary, numerous online media outlets and social media users in South-East and Central Europe referred to Khelif, who has lived as a female since birth, as a "biological man", and used male pronouns for her. Social media users then began calling for Khelif’s disqualification from the Games.

Many media outlets published false information about Khelif being a biological man. This misinformation quickly went viral, sparking yet more outraged and aggressive comments from social media users.

title: Olympics Marred by Online Hate Speech Targeting LGBTQ+ People | Balkan Insight
author: BIRN
description: The Paris Olympics was a thrilling sporting spectacle – but the opening ceremony and Algerian boxer Imane Khelif became the focus for abuse on social media that also targeted the LGBTQ+ community.
